Core Concepts in Classical Psychoanalysis: Clinical, Research Evidence and Conceptual Critiques by Morris N. Eagle. Routledge, 2017. 324 pp., $39.16 (ISBN 9781138842502).

John Auerbach PHD

John Auerbach

Gainesville, Florida, United States

619 South Marion Avenue, Lake City, FL 32025, United States
john.auerbach@va.gov
https://orcid.org/0000-0001-8390-9786

John Auerbach  

Within psychoanalysis, there are few commentators as trenchant and insightful as Morris Eagle. In fact, it has been that way for decades, beginning perhaps with his Recent Developments in Psychoanalysis: A Critical Evaluation (Eagle, 1984), although I would go further back to his early theoretical articles in the 1970s and early 1980s. Prior to then, most of Eagle's work had been in studies of psychodynamic aspects of cognition for the Research Center for Mental Health at New York University, which had been led by George Klein and Robert Holt, a biographical fact that I mention here to underscore, with reference to the volume under review, Core Concepts in Classical Psychoanalysis, that his understanding of research on unconscious mental processes is in fact considerable. And when Eagle turned to his groundbreaking critiques of classical psychoanalysis, he was walking in the footsteps of the great post-Rapaport figures like Holt (1989), Klein (1976), Gill (1994), and Schafer (1976), another fact that I mention not only because the importance of the Rapaport tradition is in danger of being lost to readers of this journal but also to point out the significance of his intellectual pedigree. Eagle's views over the years, as this review will document, are perhaps most similar to those of Holt in his attempt to maintain a connection to highly revised metapsychology, rather than to reject metapsychology altogether in the manner of George Klein, Gill, and Schafer, also in his attempt to ground his critique of psychoanalytic ideas in current scientific research. Although his efforts in this regard go back on www.pep-web.org to 1973, I would particularly note his “Interests as Object Relations” (Eagle, 1981), written at the dawn of the relational age and his first article to articulate a clear theoretical view of his own, rather a critique of others' views. In this magisterial article, also available as Eagle (1983), he argues—this was before Greenberg and Mitchell's (1983) groundbreaking summation of object relations theories in psychoanalysis, before D. N. Stern's (1985) demolition of traditional psychoanalytic developmental psychology, and also before Main et al.'s (1985) famous move to the level of representation in the conceptualization of adult attachment—that there is much empirical evidence for attachment theory, and for Fairbairn's (1952) famous dictum that the libido is object seeking—and comparatively little evidence for the traditional psychoanalytic drive theory, which considers attachment either to be derivative from sexuality as an aim inhibition (Freud, 1905/1953) or to be subsumable, in Freud's (1920/1955) final drive theory, under Eros or the life drives.

Big Moves Toward Integration: Sheldon Bach's Framework for the Treatment of Narcissistic Disorders

Jenny Kaufmann PHD and Peter Kaufmann PHD

Peter Kaufmann

115 Henry Street, #1F, Brooklyn, NY 11201, United States
Email: j.kaufmann@wawhite.org

Peter Kaufmann  

narcissistic disorders, self and object Freudians, Broad Strokes blueprint, empathic validation, integration

It's a pleasure to write this article to honor Shelly Bach and his immense contribution to psychoanalysis. For us—as psychoanalysts who came into the field in the late 80s and early 90s—Bach's ideas about understanding and treating narcissistic disorders have been immeasurably helpful and influential. This period in American psychoanalysis—known as the relational turn—emphasized perspectivalism—the influential role of the analyst's subjectivity on the treatment process—as well as the effort to integrate different theoretical perspectives within an inclusive psychoanalytic framework. Bach was in tune with these shifts and helped to broaden the Freudian tradition by bringing Kohut and Winnicott into a broader Freudian perspective that highlighted developmental considerations and placed a greater emphasis on how individual patients evolve and develop their sense of self. Along with other self and object Freudians from the New York University Postdoctoral Program (Ellman, 1998), Bach helped to reform the Freudian tradition and break the stranglehold that the classical position and ego psychology had had on American psychoanalysis in the 50s and early 60s. Bach's particular focus was on understanding and treating narcissistic disorders. He provided a Broad Strokes blueprint for the analyst by combining empathic validation of the patient's perspective while maintaining a more objective perspective about the overall treatment process and how it was unfolding so the patient could experience different aspects of themselves and become more richly integrated.

A Child is Being Murdered: A Contemporary Psychoanalytic Treatment of a Compulsion to Child Pornography

Robert Grossmark PHD

Robert Grossmark

241 Central Park West, Suite 1A, New York, NY 10024, United States
robertgrossmark@gmail.com

Robert Grossmark  

child pornography, psychoanalysis, sadomasochism, Sheldon Bach, destructiveness

I will describe the treatment of a man who was afflicted with a compulsion to child pornography. I will outline the course of the treatment from the initial manifestations of early nonsy mbolized trauma in the register of enactment to the growth of understanding, reflection, and healing. Leaning on Sheldon Bach's work, in particular, his article, “A Dream of the Maquis de Sade,” I will describe how the patient was compelled to perverse attempts to repeat in an attempt to mourn the devastations and confusions of his early years and the erasure and murder of his self. The treatment hinged on the patient's recognition that the scenarios of child pornography manifested the destruction of himself and the double identifications with both the child victims, manipulated into a perverse reversal involving pleasure in violation and pain, and with the manic omnipotence of the perpetrators. The treatment promoted the restitution and recovery of his self and his going-on-being in time and space and above all, his humanity. I will argue that a psychoanalytic treatment offers the only true help for these men who are predominantly coerced into regulatory, punitive, and purely behavioral treatments that often repeat their original and damaging traumas.

Mothers, Daughters, and Hatred: The Void in the Mirror

Carina Grossmark PHD

Carina Grossmark

55 East 87th Street, Suite 1B, New York, NY 10128, United States
Email: cgrossmark@gmail.com

Carina Grossmark  

mothers, daughters, void, dead mother, hatred

This article addresses the question of early maternal emotional absence, maternal hatred and its impact on the relationship between mothers and daughters. I will focus on adult female patients who grew up with mothers whose personalities were organized around a psychotic core—often subtle and hidden—and who mirrored a sense of self shrouded by emotional absence and hatred whenever their daughters could not intuitively meet their needs. These mothers desperately need to merge and be magically rescued and relieved from their own unbearable pain, fears, and misery. I integrate Andre Green's concepts of the dead mother and the negative, Winnicott' s foundational concept of the mother's mirror role, and Sheldon Bach's focus on gaps in states of consciousness experienced as “psychic death.” I will address some of the developmental aspects that color the psychology of these women and the deadness that lies behind the fused, disorganizing, and all-consuming dynamics between these mothers and their daughters. Finally, I will address some of the clinical challenges we encounter when working with these women.

The Utility of the Self-Other Narrative Evaluation Scale (SONES) for Assessing Internal Representations in Interpersonal Narratives

Caleb Siefert PHD , Lena Rammouni BS , Callie Jowers MS and Jenelle Slavin-Mulford PHD

Jenelle Slavin-Mulford

CASL Building, 4901 Evergreen Road, Dearborn, MI 48128, United States.
Email: csiefert@umich.edu
https://orcid.org/0000-0003-2255-2845

Jenelle Slavin-Mulford  

narrative assessment, internal representations, multimethod assessment, Social Cognition and Object Relations Scale-Global Rating Method, autobiographical memory

Many psychoanalytically informed theories (e.g., attachment theory, object relations theory) view internal representations for self and others as key personality components. Systems for assessing internal representations using narratives often rely on expert raters. Respondents do not typically provide ratings for their own narratives. This article introduces the Self-Other Narrative Evaluation Scale (SONES), which elicits respondent ratings for how they feel interpersonal events in a narrative impacted their representations of self and others. The purpose of this article is to explore the psychometric adequacy, convergent validity, and construct validity of this newly created scale. In two studies, participants provided autobiographical narratives of interpersonal interactions and rated them with the SONES. Experts independently rated all narratives with the Social Cognition and Object Relations Scale-Global Rating Method. Participants also rated their dispositional subjective well-being, self-esteem, and interpersonal problems. SONES scales were internally consistent; converged with the Social Cognition and Object Relations Scale-Global Rating Method Affective-Relational factor and Self factor; were related to narratives' emotional tone ratings; and were associated with dispositional ratings of self-esteem, subjective well-being, and interpersonal problems. Overall, the findings provide preliminary support for the validity and utility of the SONES. Implications of the study are described, limitations are noted, and future directions for studying the SONES are suggested.

The Authority of Tenderness: Dignity and the True Self, by Paul Williams, London: Routledge, 2022, 108 pp., $15.96 (ISBN 9781032009360).

William Cornell MA

William Cornell

https://orcid.org/0000-0002-5817-6338
TSTA, 145 44th Street, Pittsburgh, PA 15201, United States
Email: wfcornell@gmail.com

William Cornell  

The Authority of Tenderness: Dignity and the True Self is the third in Paul Williams' remarkable trilogy—following The Fifth Principle (Williams, 2010a) and Scum (Williams, 2014a)—writings that are not easily labeled. While compelling as texts written by a psychoanalyst, they are not psychoanalytic books as such. Although each is a deeply personal expression of battered and profoundly struggling selves, they are not exactly memoirs or autobiographies. They are something else. Williams, in his preface to this third book, characterizes his intention in the first two volumes:

The author undertook, on behalf of the subject [Williams himself], to provide a faithful, intelligible rendering of unintelligible events. The mind in question, insofar as it resembles other minds, was given an opportunity to speak to the reader, which could not have happened in reality. … (Williams, 2022, p. xii)

Comprised of a mere 85 pages, it becomes quickly apparent that, in The Authority of Tenderness, there are no superfluous words or ideas. Rather, they give voice to psychic worlds that all too often have become unbearable and unspoken, within both families of origin and our professional communities, such that being “Gone” becomes the only option for psychic survival:

Who cares anyway? The Gone child walks, turns up, leaves, goes ‘home’, day after day, week after week, doesn't it? What is there to say? (Williams, 2022, p. 43)

The chapters in this book shift among autobiography, discussions of the nature of solitude, accounts of the raw realities of minds destroyed by neglect and violence, clinical stories, and descriptions of “the authority of tenderness” in the treatment of severe psychic distress and disintegration. The chapters are not presented in any obviously linear sequence. In fact, I found myself initially taken aback by the structure of the book. But with repeated readings, I found that Williams' chapter sequencing served as a reminder of the realities of the shifting nature of a psychoanalytic treatment, between periods of understanding and potential consolidation, followed by periods of retreat or disintegration. His chapter on “Isolation Revisited” is followed by “Terror” and “Feral”; “Strokes of Fortune” is followed by “Gone” and “Shame”—seeming to offer a sober grounding in the challenges and vulnerabilities for psychic growth under these life circumstances.

[[RunningHead]]

The Monsters In-Between

John Rosegrant PHD

John Rosegrant

410 N. Huron Street, Ypsilanti, MI 48197, United States
Email: rosegrantj@gmail.com

John Rosegrant  

Sheldon Bach, monsters, transitional space

Sheldon Bach was interested in monsters as “the exotic inhabitants of that mental territory that stretches between the self and the other, between the past and the future, between the known and the unknown.” (Language of Perversion pp. 136-137) He was primarily interested in the maternal relationship as the breeding ground of monsters, but briefly pointed to the importance of the paternal relationship as well. By looking at clinical material and the life of J.R.R. Tolkien, I will develop Bach's thoughts about monsters in two directions. First, I will discuss the importance of his language that calls on myth and the arts rather than scientistic terms, thus not only producing clear writing but also saying something essential about clinical work: To help patients with their monsters, it is necessary to join them in transitional space with its “exotic inhabitants.” Second, while maintaining the centrality of the maternal relationship, I will further develop the importance of the paternal relationship in creating monsters.

Carolyn Ellman PHD

Carolyn Ellman

140 Riverside Drive, New York, NY 10024, United States
Email: cellman174@aol.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0001-6461-9599

New York, New York, United States

Carolyn Ellman  

Ferenczi, psychoanalytic politics, technique, analytic love, exclusion

Psychoanalytic politics is explored starting with Freud's death in 1939 and the politics of exclusion. The author looks at American psychoanalysis during the 70s and 80s to see what changes were taking place in technique and why the Freudians didn't embrace Ferenczi's work. When Ferenczi's work did became available in the 80s, Relational and Interpersonal schools quickly took him as their forefather. The author tries to show why the psychology of small narcissistic differences, led Freudians to keep excluding him. Using Bach, Stone, Loewald and others as examples, she shows how hard it was to influence the established way of working with patients. Through a discussion of how Bach worked with her, the author finds a way to honor both Ferenczi and Bach and their loving care of patients.

Phyllis Beren  

Psychoanalysis, Sheldon Bach, personal reflection

This essay is on the life and work of Sheldon Bach. It aims at showing how his background, talents, education, and life experience informed his theoretical thinking, his writings, and his work as a clinician. He lived over nine decades actively committed to his chosen profession. As a keen observer of the immense changes in psychoanalysis since Freud, his writings and clinical works speak to his significant contributions to evolving psychoanalytic thinking and practice.

Children's Intrapsychic Processes and Aggressive-Defiance Behaviors as a Function of Sex

Anielle Fredman MPhil , Sasha Rudenstine PHD and Steve Tuber PHD

Steve Tuber

160 Convent Avenue, North Academic Center 8/122B, New York, NY 10031, United States
Email: aniellefr@gmail.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-6821-7068

https://orcid.org/0000-0002-7659-0874

Steve Tuber  

Rorschach Inkblot test, human movement, object relations, sex, aggressive-defiance behavior

The present study investigates the relationship among intrapsychic processes and aggressive-defiance behaviors (e.g., oppositionality, bullying and destructive tendencies) as influenced by biological sex. This study seeks to gain insight into the nature of the relationship between aspects of object relations (operationalized as the proportion of human movement responses on the Rorschach Inkblot test) and Defiance/Aggression (measured by youth self-report [SR]), by examining the moderating effect of sex. Results are based on a sample of youth (N = 62, 55% male, 45% female, 27% White, 34% Black, 21% Latine, 17% other, 68% below median income) ages 7-17, receiving mental health services at a community-based clinic in an underresourced urban population. Outcomes from the present study support the hypothesis that a child's sex may influence the relationship between aspects of object relations and aggressive-defiance behaviors; specifically, for females but not for males, higher levels of internalized images (an aspect of object relations) predicted less self-reported Defiance/Aggression. Broadly, these results suggest differences in the interaction of unconscious processes and aggressive-defiance behaviors for male and female youth. Furthermore, this study offers an integration of psychoanalytic theory with test data and demonstrates the applicability of personality assessment to treatment conceptualization.

[[RunningHead]]

Referential Processes in Dreams: A Brief Report From a COVID-19 Dreams Analysis

Rachele Mariani PHD , Silvia Monaco MS , Alice Bincoletto MS , Michela Di Trani PHD , Vittorio Lingiardi MD and Guido Giovanardi PHD

Guido Giovanardi

Via Dei Marsi 75, Rome 00185, Italy.
Email: Rachele.mariani@uniroma1.it
https://orcid.org/0000-0001-5952-3525

Guido Giovanardi  

dreams, COVID-19, collective trauma, referential process, LIWC

The COVID-19 pandemic has been a collective trauma for the world population. Psychoanalytic literature has long proposed that dreams are a key point of access for understanding the deepest functioning of the mind. Using the multiple code theory (Bucci, 1993) as a framework, this study aims to explore the emotional and cognitive responses of the general population to COVID-19 through their dreams. A total of 613 dreams were collected in an online survey. Linguistic measures of referential process and a cluster analysis were performed, then one-way analyses of variance explored the differences in content among dreams' clusters according to the Linguistic Inquiry and Word Count (LIWC). Analysis suggested three dream clusters: Cluster A (N = 255) defines an arousal activation; Cluster B (N = 121) defines a phase of symbolization; Cluster C (N = 237) defines a phase of reflection/reorganization. The content analysis of LIWC presents significant differences among the three clusters (p < .05): Cluster A, compared to Cluster C, included less use of the term “we,” more frequent reference to others, and more frequent use of words denoting certainty; Cluster B included more words of negation and more use of both the present tense and the verb “to have”; Cluster C included less use of words referring to wishes and illusions. These results show differentiated functions of dreams in response to the pandemic experience, underlining the importance of dreams in the processing of this collective trauma.

[[RunningHead]]

Negative Feelings Toward Borderline Patients: Are Layperson Emotional Reactions Disorder-Specific?

Christian Sell PHD and Cord Benecke PHD

Cord Benecke

Stromstraße 3b, 10555 Berlin, Germany.
Email: christian.sell@ipu-berlin.de
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-8460-3946

https://orcid.org/0000-0002-7977-5102

Cord Benecke  

borderline personality disorder, levels of structural integration, personality functioning, countertransference, emotional reactions

Patients with borderline personality disorder (BPD) are typically confronted with others having negative emotional reactions toward them. This is especially the case with therapeutic laypersons in the health care system as well as in their social surroundings. It is unclear, however, whether these reactions are specific to BPD or associated more broadly with poor ego functioning. In this experimental analogue study, therapeutic laypersons (N = 147) were asked to watch 20-min video segments showing different types of patients talking about an interpersonal conflict. There were three conditions: (a) patients with BPD and low levels of structural integration (LSI), (b) patients without any cluster B personality disorder and with low LSI, and (c) patients without any cluster B personality disorder and moderate LSI. Axis-I-diagnoses of all patients were mood and/or anxiety disorders. The videos and their sequence were counterbalanced across participants. After each video, participants responded to the Emotional Experience and Emotion Regulation Questionnaire (EER) and the Operationalized Psychodynamic Diagnosis—Interpersonal Relations Axis (OPD-IRA). Compared to patients with moderate LSI, patients with BPD as well as other patients with low LSI evoked more active negative emotions among participants, and patients with BPD evoked more self-perceived tendencies to engage in negative interpersonal behavior. There were no significant differences in reactions toward patients with low LSI with or without BPD. Results indicate that negative emotional reactions might be associated more broadly with low ego functioning rather than being specific to BPD.

Ana García-Blanco  

differentiation of self, schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, major depression disorder, social functioning

Differentiation of self, which is understood to be the psychological individuation of a person from their parental figures, is considered a key factor in the origin of severe psychopathology. In this study, we aim to explore the ways in which the process of differentiation of self develops in people with various severe mental disorders, examining its impact on social functioning together with diagnosis, current symptoms, illness variables, and sociodemographic features. One hundred thirty patients (45 diagnosed with schizophrenia, 44 with bipolar disorder, and 41 with major depressive disorder) and 45 controls were assessed using the Differentiation of Self-Inventory-Revised, the Family of Origin Scale, the 90 Symptom Check List-Revised, and the Social Adaptation Self-Evaluation Scale. As for differentiation of self, patients showed lower I-position and poorer emotional reactivity than controls. Additionally, emotional reactivity was poorer for patients with schizophrenia and bipolar disorder than for those with major depressive disorder; and a poor emotional cutoff (remoteness) was evidenced only in schizophrenia. Individuals with schizophrenia and bipolar disorder had lower capacity for differentiation than controls, and those with schizophrenia were less differentiated than those with major depressive disorders. Among the main predictors of poor social functioning were low differentiation of self and low differentiation from the family of origin rather than any specific clinical diagnosis. These findings suggest a possible utility of using the concept of differentiation of self as a psychological marker to classify severe mental disorders. Critically, social functioning may improve with interventions focused on differentiation of self.

Amy Levy PsyD

Amy Levy

1829 East Franklin Street Unit 1200C, Chapel Hill, NC 27514, United States
Email: DrAmyLevy@DrAmyLevy.com

Amy Levy  

“The Sadness Will Last Forever”—Vincent Van Gogh

Atlas' book Emotional Inheritance: A Therapist, Her Patients, and the Legacy of Trauma (Little, Brown Spark, 2022) reasserts the phenomenon of intergenerational transmission of trauma for the psychoanalytic community and introduces the concept to the public. In the tradition of clinicians who address mainstream audiences such as Irvine Yalom, Susie Orbach, Lori Gottlieb, Stephen Grosz, and Julia Samuel the book comprised engaging and poignant treatment tales designed to entice readers into the therapeutic relationship. Atlas's theme is the working-through of intergenerational trauma, which she packages simply as “emotional inheritance.” In the language of popular psychology, Atlas explains how our ancestors' unacknowledged and/or unprocessed trauma seeks representation in later generations and reassures us that, “demons tend to vanish when we turn on the lights” (p. 71).

Like Grosz (2013), the terrain into which Atlas guides the public is psychoanalysis. She demonstrates unconscious communication—repeatedly describing how feeling states and conflicts silently pass from parent to child across generations—and conveys how the unconscious shapes our lives.

… [family] secrets … often keep us from living to our full potential. They affect our mental and physical health, create gaps between what we want for ourselves and what we are able to have, and haunt us like ghosts (p. 4).

She nods to Bollas' term “unthought known” (1987) and attempts to sell the Freudian conceptualization of mourning (1917) with the promise of ending cycles of trauma and violence.

No stranger to grief, Atlas acknowledges that the illness and passing of her life partner (Lewis Aaron) were elements with which she was absorbed during the creation of the book. Further, she grew up in Israel, where the personal and social implications of war and death were ever present in her development. Her parents were both children when their families immigrated to Israel. Their experiences were laden with loss, illness, poverty, trauma and the pain of racism and immigration which were not spoken of.

John Rosegrant PHD

John Rosegrant

New Orleans, Louisiana
410 North Huron Street, Ypsilanti, MI 48197, United States
Email: rosegrantj@gmail.com

John Rosegrant  

Fromm orients the reader in his Introduction that his “focus will be on stories of trauma: the conditions that lead to it, the forms it takes, the way it affects a person's lives and the lives of others, and how that fallout might be addressed” (p. xix). “Following Erikson, this book is not a theoretical work on trauma, even less a comprehensive survey of the field. Rather, it is a way of looking at the stories I have come across …” (p. xxv). Many of Fromm's stories come from his experiences at the Austen Riggs Center, especially participating in Group Relations Conferences, “Intensive group experiences, designed to study leadership and authority in the unfolding dynamic of the conference itself” (p. 55), that were conducted with groups dealing with unmetabolized trauma.

An ethos of insightful caring pervades this book, consistent with the approaches of Erikson and Winnicott who are guiding spirits for Fromm. This ethos is captured by certain comments and quotations that Fromm finds crucial enough to include more than once. Thus, troubled systems must be addressed with a holding environment or maternal authority that takes a “How are they right?” stance (pp. xxxvi, 66). From Winnicott's work Fromm recognizes as important factors leading to fear of breakdown both that breakdown already happened, but at an early developmental level before the child had a coherent self that could process it (pp. 5, 98, 134) and that nothing happened at a crucial developmental moment when something—mutuality with the caretaking object—might have happened (pp. 27, 205). And he quotes Adam Phillips that “People are only ever as mad as the other person is deaf” (pp. 81, 142; original in Phillips, 1996).

Fromm applies his way of looking to a diversity of stories, beginning with a section on Trauma in Families in which he explores the lives of Christopher McCandless, best known from Krakauer's Into the Wild (1996), and Erik Erikson himself. In his next section, Trauma in Organizations, Fromm explores college suicide and strains within mental health and psychoanalytic organizations. In his third and largest section, Trauma in Society, he explores post-Holocaust perpetrator trauma in modern Germany, and trauma to both perpetrator and victim in Austria; jihad; 9/11; combat trauma; the Israel/Palestine dilemma; Northern Ireland after The Troubles; and the Covid pandemic.

Email: peterwolson@earthlink.net

Peter Wolson  

John Steiner's “Illusion, Disillusion and Irony in Psychoanalysis” is a fascinating, provocative, sophisticated book in which the author explores the importance of the title's seminal psychodynamic concepts in human psychology, development, and clinical work. In contrast to focusing on case material, although he sporadically includes samples of his previously published cases, Steiner illustrates the human and clinical relevance of these concepts in classical literature, including Milton's “Paradise Lost,” Ibsen's play, “The Wild Duck,” Sophocles' tragedy, “Oedipus, the King,” Cervantes' “Don Quixote,” Keats's poem “Ode to a Nightingale,” and excerpts from other classical authors, including Shakespeare. There was so much rich material that, unfortunately, I could not cover all of it in this review.

The book provoked me into a dialog with Steiner's view of these concepts in a way that few other books have. I have deeply contemplated many of the ideas he considers over the course of my clinical career. The metaphorical illustrations from literature are powerful and, as I read the book, I found myself at times applying his clinical wisdom to my patients with therapeutic effect.

Steiner based Illusion, Disillusion, and Irony in Psychoanalysis on a series of his professional articles dating from 2013 to 2020. He investigates, among other subjects, the clinical importance of truth versus illusion, the necessity of disillusion and the acceptance of reality for psychological growth, and the extent to which irony, the capacity to look at something from multiple perspectives, is necessary for adaptative psychological functioning as well as progress in psychoanalysis. The fact that John Steiner, a highly regarded Kleinian analyst for his classic work, Psychic Retreats (1993) and other writings, is not a rigid intrapsychic analyst was refreshing. He alluded to the therapeutic impact of relational interaction throughout, especially in his discussion of the infant's omnipotent possession of the breast, an illusion that is necessary before disillusion can lead to mourning and the breast's subsequent internalization and installation as the main internal source of creativity, generosity, and nurturance within the baby's psyche (Klein, 1957). This implicitly suggested that analysts should allow patients to experience illusions, such as the primitive illusion of sufficiently having one's way with the analyst as a maternal representation before they are psychologically ready for disillusionment. The infant's illusion of possessing the breast is ended with its recognition of the mother's independent life that challenges its belief in omnipotent possession. This frequently occurs in primitive transferences as patients experience the analyst-mother's independence and mourn their loss of omnipotent control.

[[RunningHead]]

Mentalized Affectivity as a Mediator Between Two Forms of Narcissism and Self-Compassion

Alana Goodwin BA and Andrew Luchner PsyD

Andrew Luchner

1000 Holt Avenue, Winter Park, FL 32789, United States
algoodwin@hartford.edu
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-0255-8687

https://orcid.org/0000-0002-0161-5336

Andrew Luchner  

mentalized affectivity, self-compassion, grandiose narcissism, vulnerable narcissism, mediation

Mentalization, or the process used to make sense of both other people and us, implicitly and explicitly, through the context of subjective states and mental processes (Bateman & Fonagy, 2010) is not being used more often in therapeutic settings and research. One specific type of mentalization that considers the regulatory processes of emotions is mentalized affectivity (MA). MA examines the identifying, processing, and expressing aspects of emotion. In prior research, the relationship between MA and narcissism has been examined using mentalization-based treatment techniques to increase attachment relationships (Drozek & Unruh, 2020), and there is no extensive research on the relationship between MA and vulnerable and grandiose narcissism. Furthermore, the relationship between MA and self-compassion, something that people with narcissism struggle with, has not been extensively examined. The purpose of this study was to examine the relationship between narcissism (grandiose and vulnerable) and self-compassion and if MA acts as a pathway to becoming a more self-compassionate individual. A total of 161 participants completed questionnaires measuring components of both forms of narcissism, self-compassion, and MA. The parallel and simple mediation analyses found that the processing component of MA significantly influenced the relationship between both forms of narcissism and self-compassion. Future research should examine the use of mentalization in clinical populations who have been diagnosed with grandiose or vulnerable narcissism, placing an emphasis on the processing component of the model.

In three recent books, the American psychoanalyst Fred Busch puts forth a clear, rigorous, open-minded vision of contemporary psychoanalysis and makes a powerful case for its enduring relevance in 21st century life. Firmly grounded in the American Freudian tradition, Busch's work emphasizes the gradual working through of unconscious conflict and the bolstering of the mind's self-reflective capacities. In the introduction of A Fresh Look at Psychoanalytic Technique, an engaging and wide-ranging collection of clinically focused articles, originally published over a span of nearly 3 decades, Busch spells out his own dominant theoretical leaning: “The role of the ego as the arbiter of what is allowed in the patient's mind is the centerpiece of my thinking, along with its function in modulating anxiety” (2022, p. 3).

His approach is deeply indebted to the work of Paul Gray who developed a tightly focused method for analyzing the ego's unconscious defensive maneuvers. From Gray, Busch has learned to attend “to the process of associations” and to what Gray called “breaking points,” which are subtle or not-so-subtle shifts or interruptions in the patient's free associations, for it is here on the surface of speech that the analyst can observe defensive processes in action (2022, p. 71). Once a resistance can be observed, it begins to become more conscious, and through analysis, the anxiety that mobilizes it can be lessened over time, and the unconscious wishes and fantasies underlying it can be brought to the surface of awareness. Busch warns about the dangers of the analyst seeking to bypass resistance in order to bring the unconscious fantasy to light and in so doing getting beyond what the patient is ready and able to hear:

If we believe a patient is warding off something from consciousness, it is not our task to only bring this to their awareness. From the side of the ego, there is a perfectly good reason why it is being warded off; understanding this reason is a first step toward conscious acceptance of that which is being warded off. (2022, p. 38)

James Anderson  

All the candidates I knew agreed, when I was in training at the Chicago Psychoanalytic Institute, that the most valuable courses were the case conferences, in which we heard word-for-word what the analyst and the patient said during sessions. Surprisingly, it is rare to find books that present such material. In When Suicide Beckons, Bhaskar Sripada provides a verbatim account, based on recordings, of select sessions from the first 15 months of a 3-year psychoanalysis he conducted with a patient called Jan (some of the early sessions took place before the recording began and were reconstructed by Sripada from his notes). The only other book (Dewald, 1972) I know of that provides similar material was published more than 50 years ago and is long out of print.

Sripada's book would be of great value if it merely presented these forty-some sessions. But the book does much more. Sripada provides an account of the thoughts he had as he conducted the sessions. He tells us about his reactions to the patient and discusses how he decides on the comments he makes. He also offers a sort of embedded memoir; he discusses his own life experiences as his interactions with the patient bring them to his mind. And there is yet something else. He describes the basic concepts of psychoanalysis and how psychoanalytic treatment works. These descriptions will not be of special interest to most readers of Psychoanalytic Psychology, but they will be useful for students starting to learn about psychoanalysis and for nonprofessionals who read the volume.

There is yet one more advantage to the book. In keeping with today's interest in how cultural backgrounds influence psychotherapists and patients, four cultures play an important role in the book. Sripada, from an Indian, Hindu background, lived in India through medical school and emigrated to the United States to do his internship and his psychiatry residency. His patient Jan, who is in his late 40s, became a medical doctor in Poland and then emigrated to the United States, also to become a psychiatrist. Jan's key patient, Amir, an immigrant in America, was a man in his late 20s from a Muslim background in India. The four cultures-Hindu Indian, Polish, Muslim Indian, and, of course, American-all play a major role in the book.

Kris Yi  

It demands great spiritual resilience not to hate the hater whose foot is on your neck, and an even greater miracle of perception and charity not to teach your child to hate.

-James Baldwin, The Fire Next Time (Baldwin, 1962)

The 22 essays gathered in the edited book by Beverly Stoute and Michael Slevin feature mostly Black and White authors who share lessons drawn from their clinical, theoretical, and personal experiences with anti-Black racism and racial trauma, in personal life, consulting room, community, and from film. These essays stand out, among a plethora of contemporary works that address racism and racial traumas, for two merits: First, many of the chapters are written from a first-person perspective, with a number of contributors reflecting on their personal experiences of marginalization, trauma, or privilege-racial or otherwise. The personal and vulnerable nature of their writings, refreshingly free (mostly) of jargon, lends the book a feel of intimacy and relatability. Collectively, their shared experiences offer a vision of a racial comportment centered on empathic recognition of humanity and dignity in all of us, both White and Black. Second, the book offers implicit and explicit consideration, weaved throughout but especially powerfully presented by Beverly Stoute, of psychic resilience of the oppressed people. Psychoanalytic consideration of psychic resilience of racially oppressed people is a topic previously neglected in the literature; yet it is a very important topic, as it offers insights into the psychic, intergenerational, and intersubjective factors that help oppressed people survive and thrive. What can we learn from the African American community, which has withstood the nightmare of dehumanization and moral injury to survive, to carry on, and ultimately to thrive?

First, a general overview of the book. It is broken into five parts: in Part 1, Historical Perspectives, Beverly Stoute and Dionne Powell chronicle the history of race and racism in psychoanalytic theory and training. I especially liked learning about the early Black psychoanalysts' stories in American psychoanalysis. Among many racist incidents Stoute and Powell cite, Ralph Greenson's interpretation of his Black analysand Ellis Toney's request to change appointment time as Oedipal and paranoid stands out as a classic example of White psychoanalysis's pathologization of the actual, lived Black experience of persecution; Toney was merely trying to avoid police stopping him on the streets of Greenson's White neighborhood. Another story is about Margaret Morgan Lawrence, the first Black psychoanalyst to complete training in America. She faced tremendous difficulties in her analytic training, including having her fitness for graduation questioned by her institute's leadership. Stoute and Powell's telling of the stories of the early Black analysts' experiences and struggles brings recognition to the Black presence in American psychoanalysis.

Psychoanalysts' Work Throughout the COVID-19 Pandemic: An Empirical Study on Teleanalysis During the Italian Lockdown

Antonello Colli PHD , Giulia Gagliardini PHD , Raffaele Pepi PHD , Alessandra D'Agostino PHD , Salvatore Gullo PHD and Mario Monti MD

Mario Monti

Via Saffi 15, 61029 Urbino, Italy
Email: antonello.colli@uniurb.it

https://orcid.org/0000-0002-3896-162X

https://orcid.org/0000-0003-0499-8990

https://orcid.org/0000-0003-4007-6336

https://orcid.org/0000-0002-1034-7636

Mario Monti  

psychoanalysis, online therapy, COVID-19

The COVID-19 pandemic has brought several limitations in everyday life, which have, in turn, influenced psychoanalysts' work with their patients. In Italy, in March 2020, a national lockdown was applied to the population, and this has led many psychoanalysts to work remotely with their patients. The aims of this study were to analyze psychoanalysts' (N = 100) interventions' style before and during the COVID-19 lockdown and to evaluate the impact of analysts' feelings on their interventions style. Measures included the Comparative Psychotherapy Process scale, the Psychoanalytic Periodical Rating Scale, and the Feeling Word Checklist-24. Analysts rated an adult patient with no psychotic disorder whom they saw before (in-person) and during (remotely) the lockdown. Mixed robust analysis of variance showed that there were statistically significant differences in psychoanalysts' interventions before and during the lockdown. These differences were also related to patients' level of functioning, with an increase in cognitive behavioral (CB) interventions with neurotic patients during the lockdown and a decrease in interventions (both CB or psychodynamic) with borderline patients during the lockdown. Moreover, psychoanalysts tended to focus more on affects and less on unconscious experiences during the lockdown, compared to before. Results showed the presence of significantly higher levels of negative-anxious feelings during the lockdown. CB interventions were significantly predicted by positive feelings and by negative-anxious feelings. These results seem to point toward some peculiarities related to the remote work with the patients during the lockdown, which should be addressed in everyday clinical practice.

[[RunningHead]]

Computerized Linguistic Analysis of Counselors' Clinical Notes in a University Counseling Center: Which Associations Correspond With Students' Symptom Reduction in a Brief Psychodynamic Intervention?

Alexandro Fortunato PHD , Alessia Renzi PHD , Silvia Andreassi PHD , Vito Maniaci MS , Costanza Franchini MS , Mara Morelli PHD , Gaetano Sciabica PsyD , Anna Speranza PHD and Rachele Mariani PHD

Rachele Mariani

Via degli Apuli, 1, 00185 Rome, Italy
Email: alexandro.fortunato@uniroma1.it
https://orcid.org/0000-0001-5543-3805

Rachele Mariani  

psychological intervention, counseling, university students, linguistic measures, psychological symptoms

University counseling represents an effective tool for promoting students' mental health, for reducing a variety of psychological disorders, and to offer developmental support and guidance in the resolution of educational and academic challenges. Despite this, there is still a paucity of literature on which variables are involved in explaining the effectiveness of university counseling. For this reason, we applied the multiple code theory to the clinical process by analyzing therapists' clinical notes. Aims of the study are to investigate the usefulness of a university counseling intervention by evaluating changes in the psychological symptomatology between pre- and postintervention and in the linguistic measures applied to clinical reports of the first and last sessions. The sample consisted of 88 university students (mean age = 23.30, 73.9% identified as female) and 15 psychologists (mean age = 33.93). Measures used are Italian Discourse Attributes Analysis Program and Linguistic Measures of the Referential Process, Outcome Questionnaire-45, Beck Anxiety Inventory, Beck Depression Inventory-II, Personality Inventory for Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders-5 Brief Form. Results showed a significant reduction in patients' symptomatology in all the dimensions investigated. Moreover, some significant changes in linguistic measures applied to clinical reports emerged such as an increase in reflection/reorganization process. Finally, these findings are also confirmed by significant correlations between students' personality traits and linguistic measures of clinical reports, between pre/postintervention tests scores, and linguistic characteristics of first/last clinical reports that respectively emerged. In conclusion, our study shows that brief counseling intervention shows improvement in students' symptoms by both investigating the instruments and analyzing the writing style of counselors who performed the brief treatments.

[[RunningHead]]

Rorschach Assessment of Ego Involvement in Affect Regulation: Interrater Reliability of Form Dominance in Shading and Achromatic Color Responses

Anthony Bram PHD , Donald Viglione PHD , Oren Lee-Parritz PsyD , Kiley Gottschalk PsyD , Jed Yalof PsyD , James Kleiger PsyD , Ksera Dyette PsyD and Ali Khadivi PHD

Ali Khadivi

329 Massachusetts Avenue, No. 2, Lexington, MA 02420, United States
Email: Anthony_Bram@hms.harvard.edu
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-5178-1666

Newton, Massachusetts, United States

San Diego

Boston, Massachusetts, United States

Brookline, Massachusetts, United States

Stockbridge, Massachusetts, United States

Bethesda, Maryland, United States

Ali Khadivi  

rorschach, ego involvement, affect regulation, form dominance

Although emphasis on psychodynamic thinking has waned in assessment training, the ascendant Rorschach Performance Assessment System (R-PAS; Meyer et al., 2011) has reintegrated psychoanalytic concepts into empirical Rorschach assessment: R-PAS adds scores involving object relations, implicit dependency, aggressive ideation, and ego impairment. R-PAS has, however, excluded the psychodynamic framework for assessing ego involvement in the regulation of anxiety/dysphoria by eliminating the coding of Form Dominance in Shading and Achromatic Color (FDSHAC) that has been part of the Comprehensive System (Exner, 2003). This decision was based in part on concerns about efficiently and reliably coding distinctions among Form Dominant, Form Secondary, and Formless levels of FDSHAC. To establish that such distinctions can be coded reliably, we applied supplemental guidelines (Viglione, 2010) to evaluate reliability among four experienced assessors who coded determinants for 155 Rorschach responses, 115 of which required FDSHAC determination. Applying Gwet's AC2′s to ordinal scales, interrater reliabilities were good to excellent. Reliabilities were strongest for Form Dominance in Texture and Achromatic Color, modestly so for Form Dominance in Diffuse Shading, and problematic for Form Dominance of Vista. Among levels of Form involvement across FDSHAC variables, raters had the most difficulty distinguishing Form Secondary. We discuss considerations for clinical coding, psychodynamic configurational analyses for interpretation, and construct validation research.

[[RunningHead]]

Knowing Me, Knowing You: A Systematic Review of Object Relations Assessment

Caterina Felici MSc , Fabio Madeddu MD , Stephan Doering MD , John Clarkin PHD and Emanuele Preti PHD

Emanuele Preti

Piazza dell'Ateneo Nuovo 1, 20126 Milano (MI), Italy
Email: c.felici1@campus.unimib.it
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-3777-2747

https://orcid.org/0000-0003-3768-770X

https://orcid.org/0000-0001-7455-1380

https://orcid.org/0000-0002-1805-9508

https://orcid.org/0000-0001-5207-4579

Emanuele Preti  

object relations, assessment, personality disorders

Tools for assessing object relations (ORs) are fundamental for psychodynamic theory, research, and practice. ORs play an important role in diagnostic and therapeutic processes, helping therapists to better understand and manage complex relational functioning in their patients. The clinical relevance of OR is also recognized by the first criterion of the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, fifth edition Alternative Model for Personality Disorders. Our goal is to provide clinicians and researchers with an updated review of the available empirical tools to assess OR. We conducted a systematic search on APA PsycInfo and Pubmed and retrieved 42 instruments from the existing literature. Some of these assessment tools are well established in the scientific community. Among the less popular ones, we identified some instruments that show good potential and may be granted a further round of validation. We encourage the development of existing and new instruments within the framework of Kernberg's theory of personality organization, and a further inquiry into qualitative aspects of ORs dyads, which may help therapists obtain more sophisticated information on their patients' relational functioning.

[[RunningHead]]

A Jungian Approach to Psychological Intervention: Using the Cultural Archetype of the “Gourd” Image to Support Chinese Frontline Medical Staff During COVID-19

Jingyu Liang PHD , Yiqing He PHD and Jingwen Zhang PHD

Jingwen Zhang

Panyu District, Guangzhou 510006, People's Republic of China
Email: Ho_Yiqing@126.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0001-8917-1268

Jingwen Zhang  

COVID-19, frontline workers, gourd (“hulu”), analytical psychology, mental health

After the sudden outbreak of COVID-19, frontline medical staff stepped forward to protect people from the virus. Medical staff have experienced many psychological problems during the pandemic, such as anxiety and depression, as well as adverse somatic reactions, such as sleep disorders and physical pain. Based on the theory and clinical practice of analytical psychology, this article proposes the image of the gourd (“hulu”), an archetype with rich cultural connotations, as a novel tool for psychological intervention. The image of the gourd has its origins in Chinese culture and is one of the most important symbols in Chinese tradition, ubiquitous in architecture (such as that of the Forbidden City), national costumes, poetry, calligraphy, and painting. Linked with the archetype of the “Great Mother,” the gourd signifies the birth and origin of life and evokes the functions of reproduction, nurturing, and protection. At the same time, the gourd is the archetype of the physician (healer) in Chinese culture, standing for physical and mental healing and the essence of health and longevity. In addition, the gourd is linked with the archetype of “temenos.” (The “temenos” is one of the important ways in which the therapeutic containment is conceptualized in analytical psychology. Jung drew the analogy between the consulting room and the temenos, the “holy of holies” at the center of the Greek Temple as a sacred and inviolate place; Abramovitch, 2002.) As an important sacred (sacrificial) vessel, the gourd is a symbolic agent of communication between heaven and earth and human beings and immortals, and it is responsible for expelling evil spirits and warding off disasters. This article proposes the image of the gourd as a novel tool for providing psychological support for frontline medical staff during the COVID-19. The trauma of frontline staff can be subsumed within the image of the gourd, which evokes functions such as sealing, housing, protection, healing, and transformation, to restore their internal psychological resources and spiritual strength.

Samantha Carlucci PHD , Livia Chyurlia MSc , Michelle Presniak PHD , Nancy Mcquaid PHD , Stephanie Wiebe PHD , Robert Hill MA , James Wiley BA , Camille Garceau BSc , Danielle Baldwin MA , Chloe Slowikowski MA , Iryna Ivanova PHD , Renee Grenon PHD , Louise Balfour PHD , Hilary Maxwell PHD and Giorgio Tasca PHD

Giorgio Tasca

136 Jean-Jacques Lussier, Ottawa, ON, K1N 9A8, Canada
Email: scarl090@uottawa.ca

Giorgio Tasca  

binge-eating disorder, defense mechanisms/defensive functioning, defense mechanism rating scale (DMRS), validity, reliability

We assessed the reliability and validity of the Defense Mechanism Rating Scale (DMRS) when used with the Adult Attachment Interview (AAI) and compared defensive functioning among women with and without binge-eating disorder (BED). Participants were women with BED (n = 101), overweight/obese women without BED (n = 47), and normal weight women without BED (n = 49). Participants completed the AAI, and self-report measures of interpersonal distress, reflective functioning, and attachment insecurity. Coders rated the DMRS and reflective functioning from AAI transcripts. We found acceptable interrater reliability on the DMRS; significant correlations between the DMRS and interpersonal distress, reflective functioning, and attachment insecurity; and significantly lower defensive functioning among women with BED compared to normal weight women without BED. The DMRS is reliable and valid when rated from AAI transcripts. BED is associated with lower defensive functioning. Further research on defensive functioning in BED may lead to better treatment outcomes.

Robert Drozek  

Mentalization-based treatment (MBT), and the construct of mentalizing, is currently experiencing a period of dramatic growth and expansion. Mentalization is defined as the capacity to “read,” access, and reflect on mental states (e.g., thoughts, emotions, desires, attitudes, values) in ourselves and other people. While the term has been floating around within psychoanalysis since the 1950s, this particular understanding of mentalization was first proposed in 1989 by the psychoanalyst Peter Fonagy, who suggested that people with borderline personality disorder suffer from context-dependent vulnerabilities in their ability to flexibly reflect on mental states in themselves and others (Fonagy, 1989). Fonagy partnered with fellow analyst Anthony Bateman to develop and systematize MBT: an exploratory yet structured psychotherapy that integrates principles from attachment theory, developmental psychology, neuroscience, and clinical psychoanalysis to address instability in emotions, self-esteem, and interpersonal relationships (Bateman & Fonagy, 2016).

MBT has emerged as a leading evidence-based therapy for borderline personality disorder (Storebø et al., 2020), resulting in decreased depression, anger issues, paranoia, hostility, self-injury, suicidality, medication usage, and inpatient hospitalizations, as well as notable advances in social, academic, and vocational functioning (Bateman et al., 2016, 2021; Bateman & Fonagy, 1999, 2008, 2009). But the use of MBT has extended well beyond borderline personality disorder, with research supporting MBT's effectiveness in treating antisocial personality disorder, adolescent self-injury, eating disorders, posttraumatic stress disorder, and substance use disorders (Bateman & Fonagy, 2019). More recently, the reach of MBT has expanded even further, with applications being developed for pathological narcissism (Drozek & Unruh, 2020, 2022; Drozek et al., 2023), short-term psychotherapy (Juul et al., 2019), family therapy (Asen & Fonagy, 2021), law enforcement violence (Drozek et al., 2021), and virtual treatment in the context of the COVID-19 pandemic (Ventura Wurman et al., 2021). Within empirical psychology, research has clearly established the role of problems in mentalizing across various forms of psychopathology (Bora, 2021; Luyten et al., 2020), as well as the importance of parental mentalization in effective parenting and child development (Camoirano, 2017; Zeegers et al., 2017).

Ruth Zeligman  

object relations, containment, Object Relations Inventory, Rorschach

The study explored the relationship between internal representations of object relations and Bion's notion of containment in a sample of 33 adolescents and their mothers. Using performance-based measures, the study examined the association between levels of object representations as rated by the Differentiation-Relatedness (D-R) and Conceptual Level (CL) Object Relations Inventory (ORI) scales, and Rorschach measures of the capacity to contain and process emotional experiences. In addition, the study explored the intergenerational transmission of these constructs by examining the relational matrices between mothers and their adolescent children. The results indicated a positive correlation between the two ORI scales and between the D-R and Rorschach measure of imagination, empathy, and reflection, which was also negatively related to a measure of emotional dysregulation. No significant relationship was found between the adolescents and their mothers. These findings highlight the association between levels of internal representations and the capacity for containment and emotional processing.

psychoanalysis, narcissistic states, Sheldon Bach, psychoanalytic treatment, Winnicott

This article reviews the contributions of each of the authors in this Special Issue. We look at the relationship of the concepts that are developed to the work of Dr. Sheldon Bach. Most of the articles illuminate some aspect of Bach's interest in the early development and treatment of narcissistic issues and personality traits. There is also an attempt to integrate Bach's contributions and distill his implicit theory of personality development and psychoanalytic treatment. The article ends with a discussion of S. Ellman's contributions that build on and extend the work of Sheldon Bach.

Nachträglichkeit and Après-Coup in the Psychological Scientific Literature: A Systematic Review and Future Perspectives

Selene Mezzalira PHD PsyD , Emanuele Pick PHD and Arianna Palmieri PHD

Arianna Palmieri

Via Ponte Bucci Cubo 18/C, Rende, CS 87036, Italy
Email: selene.mezzalira@unical.it
https://orcid.org/0000-0003-2110-440X

https://orcid.org/0000-0003-1463-4664

https://orcid.org/0000-0003-0453-001X

Arianna Palmieri  

Nachträglichkeit/après-coup, temporal experience, trauma, clinical setting, meaning-making

The crucial role of Nachträglichkeit/après-coup (N/AC) in psychoanalytic psychology has been afforded lower consideration in the scientific literature than other relevant psychoanalytic constructs. No systematic reviews have been provided on this construct. We aimed at investigating the many facets of the psychoanalytic significance N/AC as addressed in the scientific literature, outlining its potential developments. According to the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses guidelines, we selected peer-reviewed, indexed articles based on the psychoanalytic concept of N/AC in the scientific literature. The keywords we entered in Pubmed, Web of Science, Scopus, and Psychoanalytic Electronic Publishing (PEP) were: Nachträglichkeit, après-coup, and psych*. Studies published between the 1950s and 2022 were considered. The initial database search yielded 600 results. After duplicates removal, 414 contributions were screened, of which 35 articles met the inclusion criteria. A metasynthesis of the latter revealed that N/AC is crucially involved in four different—albeit partially overlapping—domains, where N/AC respectively features as (a) a metapsychological construct explicative of the retroactive constitution of experience, (b) a cornerstone for the emergence of traumatic psychopathology, (c) a resource of the therapeutic process in clinical settings, and (d) a core facet of meaning-making processes involved in the constitution of memory. As a nonlinear, multidimensional process, N/AC is still recognized as a crucial concept in the scientific literature. Yet, no attempts were found, which investigated the phenomenon from an empirical standpoint. Based on the theoretical cues provided in the selected literature, future explorations of N/AC in terms of meaning-making processes and neuroscientific perspectives are suggested.

From Childhood Emotional Maltreatment to Disordered Eating: A Path Analysis

Alessandro Musetti PHD , Giulia Gagliardini PHD , Vittorio Lenzo PHD and Stefania Cella PHD

Stefania Cella

Borgo Carissimi 10, 43121 Parma, Italy.
Email: alessandro.musetti@unipr.it
https://orcid.org/0000-0003-4125-0068

Stefania Cella  

disordered eating, emotional maltreatment, attachment, defense styles, reflective functioning

Childhood emotional maltreatment (i.e., emotional abuse and emotional neglect), attachment, reflective functioning (RF), defense styles, and disordered eating (DE) are associated with each other. However, no study has yet investigated all these variables simultaneously. The present study aimed to test the mediating role of attachment, RF, and defense styles in the relationship between childhood emotional abuse and neglect and DE. Self-report questionnaires were completed by 769 Italian adults (48.1% females; mean age = 28.89; SD = 7.28). DE, childhood emotional abuse and neglect, attachment (anxiety and avoidance), RF, and defense styles were assessed. Bivariate correlations and path analysis were run separately for males and females. We found significant indirect effects between emotional abuse and neglect and DE via attachment insecurities (anxiety and avoidance), low RF, and neurotic and immature defense styles in females. In addition, neurotic and immature defense styles and RF mediated the relationship between emotional neglect, attachment anxiety, and DE, in males. The findings supported the hypothesized model of risk factors for DE and provided additional insight into mechanisms underlying the relationship between childhood emotional maltreatment and DE. Prevention and intervention programs should be targeted at improving attachment security, defensive functioning maturity, and RF in people with a history of childhood emotional maltreatment.

Falling Out of the World

Mary Johnston PHD

Mary Johnston

4820 South Greenwood Avenue, Chicago, IL 60615, United States
Email: hjohnston@chicagoanalysis.org
https://orcid.org/0000-0003-0891-1362

Mary Johnston  

COVID pandemic, polio, dissociation, trauma, Winnicott

This article is a personal account of how the coronavirus pandemic triggered the mobilization of childhood defenses first employed when I fell victim to the polio epidemic. The article describes commonalities between the experience of polio and my own and others’ reactions to the pandemic—the collapse of time, the lapse into numbness and passivity, the management of mortal fear through denial and dissociation, and the reentry into a changed world. When a terrible illness threatens one's body, there is a crack in the fantasy of omnipotence, of physical resilience, of a promising future. The article's title went through several iterations. The first was Staying Alive, which accurately reflected a nearly universal preoccupation of the past 3 years. The second attempt stated the core premise of the article, The Events That Change Us. The present title, Falling Out of the World, dares to name the deeper premise of the article—how traumatic events can result in our no longer having access to the world we formerly inhabited. There is no return to normal.

A Look at COVID-19 and Transgender Youth Through a Psychoanalytic Lens

Diane Ehrensaft PHD

Diane Ehrensaft

5633 Ocean View Drive, Oakland, CA 94618, United States
Email: Diane.Ehrensaft@ucsf.edu

Diane Ehrensaft  

transgender youth, COVID-19, true gender self, gender creativity, gender stress

The arrival of COVID-19 pandemic led to a much-publicized mental health crisis for American youth. A subgroup of those youth particularly affected were transgender and gender diverse (TGD) children and adolescents. Yet, a psychoanalytic eye cast on these youth revealed not a crisis, but a dialectic: from a relational and intrapsychic perspective, it was the best of times and the worst of times for TGD young people. This article applies Winnicott's concepts of mirroring and the author's constructs of the true gender self, false gender self, and gender creativity to information gleaned from survey studies and clinical observation. These data were then used to analyze why some TGD children and adolescents experienced the shutdown phase of the pandemic as an opportunity to consolidate their gender self, while others experienced the shutdown as a significant environmental impingement and threat to their gender self and overall well-being.

[[RunningHead]]

Impacts of the COVID-19 Pandemic on Pregnant and Postpartum Women: Where is the Village?

Elizabeth Fritsch PHD

Elizabeth Fritsch

6723 Whittier Avenue, Suite 307, McLean, VA 22101, United States
Email: drelizabethfritsch@gmail.com

Elizabeth Fritsch  

postpartum depression, maternal mental health, community outreach, perinatal therapy

As the COVID-19 pandemic took hold of the United States, pregnant women and mothers of infants were among those most affected by fears of the virus, the disruption of health care systems, social isolation, and the collapse of social supports. This article describes how a group of psychoanalysts and psychoanalytic therapists in a community outreach initiative, the SPRING Project, worked to meet these mental health needs by providing affordable psychotherapy at this critical time. Two case reports highlight the ways the pandemic magnified postpartum distress as well as the relevance and efficacy of a psychodynamic clinical approach in resolving such distress. In addition, the author chronicles how early in the pandemic the SPRING Project launched support groups for pregnant women. Participants in this virtual village helped offset the painful isolation of these women and supported their emotional development during their pregnancies.

Deborah Kadish MD

Deborah Kadish

4 Hartford Street, Office 304, Newton Highlands, MA 02461, United States

Deborah Kadish  

play therapy, COVID-19 pandemic, outdoor treatment

During the COVID-19 pandemic, I found Zoom to be an unsatisfying tool for treating young patients. I was able to create a backyard “office,” inviting children and preteens to continue their play therapy sessions outdoors. I review how working outside changed the therapeutic frame. I noticed shifts in boundaries and treatment. Case examples, including a composite patient with attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), demonstrate some of the themes that arose in the new setting.

[[RunningHead]]

The Monster Outside the Door: A Developmental-Psychoanalytic Exploration of the Impact of COVID-19 on the Lives of Children and Adolescents

Norka Malberg PsyD

Norka Malberg

230 S Frontage Road, New Haven, CT 06519, United States
Email: NMalberg@gmail.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-1678-4423

Norka Malberg  

mentalization, developmental psychoanalysis, children, COVID

The potential and current impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on the socioemotional functioning of children, adolescents, and their families are explored through research evidence and a developmental-psychoanalytic perspective. We depart from the pioneering efforts and lessons learned from Anna Freud's Hampstead War Nurseries about the value of clinical observation and its impact on technical modifications during moments of crisis. Furthermore, we illustrate and reflect on the benefits of the application of a mentalization-based lens during online clinical work with children, adolescents, and families. The need for technical modifications that adapt themselves to the sociocultural needs of the families we serve during moments of crisis is emphasized.

[[RunningHead]]

Trauma of the COVID-19 Pandemic in a Latency Child

David Abrams PHD

David Abrams

7110 21st Avenue, Apartment 6B, Brooklyn, NY 11204, United States
Email: david.m.abrams@gmail.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0003-2432-2058

New York, New York, United States

David Abrams  

COVID-19, children, psychoanalysis, PTSD, dreams

Article presents the online analytic treatment of Alfred, a 612-year-old African American boy in whom the outbreak and first 2 years of the COVID-19 pandemic caused a devastating posttraumatic stress disorder and chronic ongoing cumulative trauma. In contrast to concrete attacking life-threatening external objects in the case of collapsing buildings, car accidents, or rape, COVID-19 was represented by this sensitive and imaginative child as a gradually more amorphous and invisible monster that quarantined his mother away from him for 2 months, killed his great uncle, and eventually infected him with frightening asthmatic breathing. The mandated lockdown of the in-person closing of his school, after school activities, church, and neighborhood playground engendered a closed-in prison-like experience within his home, which stimulated animal phobia, agoraphobia, separation anxiety, and occasional claustrophobia. He seemed first to internally experience the COVID-19 pandemic displaced onto a fox, large dogs, and his father as an outer space alien dressed in the body suit, gloves, mask, and face shield required in his health worker military job. Then these displacements gradually changed to the more inconsistently visible bed bugs, vampires, and shape shifting monsters in his fantasies, stories, and dreams. COVID-19 turned Alfred's world from supportive childhood social relations to long periods of lonely isolation in front of his television and computer, where he was further traumatized by watching the entire video of the murder of George Floyd and the powerful metaphor of the COVID-19 pandemic in Carpenter's (1982) horror film “The Thing.”

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

“Bye, Click, and Gone”—A Qualitative Study About the Experiences of Psychotherapists and Adolescent Patients on Remote Treatment During the COVID-19 Pandemic

Susanne Benzel PHD and Alice Graneist PHD

Alice Graneist

https://orcid.org/0000-0001-6515-9803

Theodor-W.-Adorno Platz 6, Frankfurt am Main 60629, Germany
graneist@psych.uni-frankfurt.de
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-2755-5167

Alice Graneist  

remote, psychotherapy, psychodynamic, adolescents, COVID-19

Due to the high risk of infection during the COVID-19 pandemic, direct contact between patients and their therapists, which is usually considered crucial, became a potential threat. In order to prevent the disruption of psychotherapeutic treatment, many psychotherapists switched to different forms of synchronous remote video or telephone communication. However, the implementation of remote treatment was quite abrupt and led many therapists to question how the therapeutic process might continue using this technology-based communication, especially in the context of children and adolescents. Accordingly, the aim of the study was to explore psychodynamic child and adolescent psychotherapists and adolescent patients about their experiences with a remote setting. In Germany, we interviewed 24 psychodynamic child and youth therapists and 11 patients, aged 15-23 years. The qualitative analysis resulted in four themes: during remote treatment therapists and adolescent patients primarily experienced altered conditions of (a) time and space, (b) closeness and distance, (c) corporeality and body awareness, and (d) transitions. We discuss here the potentials and limitations of remote therapy with adolescents and young adults, while especially considering a developmental and adolescent theoretical perspective.

Kimberly Kleinman MS LCSW , Mary Johnston PsyD , Noya Dekel PsyD and Andrea Corn PsyD

Andrea Corn  

child & adolescent psychotherapy, psychoanalytic virtual psychotherapy, pandemic

An urgent mental health crisis has arisen as the COVID-19 pandemic continues into its third year in the United States. How do we understand children's reactions to these developmental disruptions? A group of child analytic clinicians, working with children and families online throughout the pandemic, have observed long-range consequences on development that range from traumatic disruptions to growth opportunities. Three clinical vignettes illustrate the varying impact of the move to online school and therapy on children's self-development. We consider what kinds of external and internal supports a child need in order to make use of the social interactions that occur in virtual reality.

[[RunningHead]]

The Parallel Pandemic: The Impact of COVID-19 on the Mental Health of Children With Neurocognitive Impairments

Nechama Sorscher PHD

Nechama Sorscher

127 West 96th Street, Suite 1B, New York, NY 10025, United States
Email: drsorschersoffice@gmail.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-4879-2401

Nechama Sorscher  

psychoeducation, psychology, psychoanalysis

The COVID-19 pandemic led to many months of school closures, quarantining, and social isolation for children and their families. This disruption of routine had significant implications for the mental health of children and adolescents, leading to a parallel mental health pandemic among this vulnerable population (Fegert et al., 2020). While these psychosocial effects were most acute during the initial phase, the ripples of the pandemic continue to be felt during the time of this writing (2022), as we continue to be confronted with the ever-evolving virus and its myriad aftereffects. A review of the literature suggests an increase in depression and anxiety among children and adolescents as a result of the sweeping changes to their environment (Barendse et al., 2022; Vizard et al., 2020; Waite et al., 2021). Fear of contagion, boredom, loneliness, increased social anxiety, irritability, inattention, and increased disruptive behaviors were all observed (de Figueiredo et al., 2021; Jiao et al., 2020). For children with neurocognitive impairments, the impact was even more severe due to a proliferation of factors that will be discussed below (Korpa et al., 2021). Nonetheless, despite the pandemic's pointed impact on mental health, some children and adolescents were actually able to thrive and even noted improvements in their social and emotional functioning (Chawla et al., 2021; Silk et al., 2022). This article will examine the specific impact of the pandemic on young learners with the most common types of neurocognitive disorders and provide recommendations for intervention.

Finding Opportunity in the Crisis: Executive Functioning Coaching During the Pandemic

Anna Levy-Warren PHD , Megan Gaffney MFA , Michael Ferrara PHD and Robert Cox PHD

Robert Cox

448 7th Avenue, Suite 1EL, Brooklyn, NY 11215, United States
Email: anna.levywarren@dwellnesstherapy.com

https://orcid.org/0000-0003-4324-8930

Brooklyn, New York, United States

Robert Cox  

executive functioning, learning, tutor, rapport, virtual

This article describes the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on the relationships and work between executive functioning coaches and their clients. It especially looks at the shared experience of the crisis in those relationships, and how this affected the interactions between them. How the frame, boundaries, and personal disclosures were altered is highlighted. Three case reports show the very varied ways in which the shared crisis affected the work and the two individuals engaged in it. The overall strengthening of the dyads is a surprising and positive effect of the global crisis in these relationships.

Eve Watson  

Queerness in many ways is about something being awry and off kilter, and not going smoothly, chronologically, or predictably. That can certainly be said about this book review which managed to elude this writer's grasp for longer than anticipated and thus my own sense of dislocated time permeated the reading of the text, a situation which only added to the text's success in dislocating reading from any easy positionality or normatively established apperception, requiring a readiness to be challenged by the in-between, outside, excluded, permeable, indecipherable, and the attachments to meaning that produce the socially sanctioned norms and expectations of childhood. This edited collection addresses queerness and childhood, each hefty themes in their own right, with an air of freshness built upon a foundation of psychoanalytic theory, primarily that of Freud, Lacan, and Laplanche, and impactful queer contributions since queer theory's resurgence in the 90s which sought to expand the limited identarian foci of gay and lesbian studies and challenge normativity in all aspects of human existence and not only in the arena of sexuality.

There is a timeliness to the arrival of this text as anxieties about childhood continue to resonate and permeate social bodies and are increasingly perceptible in media, policy, legal frameworks, the psychology of children, and relative to the continued diversification of family structures and changes to traditional communities. Current discourses on transgender children reflect these changes and associated tensions. The significance of the queer child is posed by the collection's editors, Anna Fishzon and Emma Lieber, who draw inspiration from Eve Sedgwick's queer textual analyses and Lee Edelman's challenge to the centralization of the child in the dominant social norm that is reproductive futurity. They ask

What can we make of this apparent dialectical synthesis of the Child and the Queer? Is the queer child a newly created object or an old one, re-found? More to the point, is the queer child the latest fetishistic invention, or has it been there all along, the specter shadowing both [Lee] Edelman's fantasmatic Child and the adult queer? (p. 5)
