J. Dean  

the ultimate claustrophobia is the syllogism

the ultimate claustrophobia is “it all adds up”

nothing adds up & nothing stands in for

anything else

—Diane di Prima, Revolutionary Letter#75(DiPrima, 2021)

I have been struck for some time now, at least since the beginning of the pandemic, if not before, by a growing interest in fairly sophisticated discussions of mental health in the media, often with an emphasis on psychoanalysis—a topic that, not so long ago, only appeared in nonprofessional publications and general bookstores because Frederick Crews had just published another iteration of his attack on Freud. But in the last few years, there have been articles in such publications as Harper's, The Baffler, n + 1, the Los Angeles Review of Books, the New York Times, and others addressing sophisticated questions related to mental health in general and psychoanalysis in particular, as well as a new collection of articles from the New Yorker by the journalist Rachel Aviv of her reporting on the problematics of psychological medicine, Strangers to Ourselves. And now, with Animal Joy: A Book of Laughter and Resuscitation, by poet and psychoanalyst Nuar Alsadir, we have a book specifically about psychoanalysis that Time magazine has announced as one of the 100 must-read books of 2022 (https://time.com/collection/must-read-books-2022/6228432/animal-joy/). The times, I have to say, they are a-changin’.

What strikes me here is growing awareness of the problematic of “mental health” as something that can be grasped through reproducible empirical research, almost certainly related to the ways in which all our ideas about normality have proven questionable, especially in light of such catastrophic events as the obvious worsening of climate change across the world, extreme economic inequality at a time when neoliberalism was supposed to have made life better for everybody—to have ended history, in fact—while a viral pandemic has devastated everyone, and ongoing wars, with the threat of nuclear violence, are on the rise. Nothing is working out the way anybody expected it to, so how can we be so sure we even know what “mental health” is?

Tilmann Habermas  

Wilma Bucci is a major force in the attempt to bridge clinical psychoanalysis and academic psychology. Her 1997 monograph Psychoanalysis and Cognitive Science: A Multiple Code Theory proposed a theory of the unconscious in terms of three levels of symbolization, reformulating psychoanalytic concepts in terms of imagery and cognitive schemata. A central concept in Bucci's theory is the emotion schema, which is based on early emotional experiences with primary caretakers. These idiosyncratic emotion schemata serve as templates for interpreting present-day experiences with others and producing emotional reactions. At the core of the theory is a model of three steps in which emotion schemata manifest, going from somatic changes such as arousal (nonsymbolic) to the evocation of images, memories, and entire stories (nonverbal symbolic), to finally a reflection on what these might mean (verbal). One way of understanding Bucci's work is that it reformulates core elements of psychoanalytic metapsychology such that it is compatible with modern psychology.

This volume gathers theoretical articles and chapters written between the publication of Bucci's milestone monograph and 2018. They are grouped into five theoretical chapters, maybe more, addressing researchers and six chapters that interpret clinical practice in terms of dual code theory. By reading the chapters in sequence, within each of the two sections, readers get a sense of the evolution of Wilma Bucci's theory. In the past 25 years, she has continued the highly ambitious program of reformulating psychoanalytic theory by couching it in terms of up-to-date theories from cognitive and emotion psychology. She confronts her thinking, for example, with Damasio's conception of emotions and discusses how dual code theory can be translated into Damasio's conceptual framework (Chapter 3) and how it can be made compatible with a variety of concepts from emotion theory and memory psychology. The concept of emotion schema is also likened to established cognitive versions of internalized object relations, such as Bowlby's internal working model and Stern's representations of interactions that have been generalised (Chapter 5); other such concepts would have been Tomkins’ nuclear script, Luborsky's core conflictual relational theme, and Horowitz’ person schema. All of these were devised to conceptualize modern psychoanalytic understandings of personal ways of relating to others in dysfunctional ways in such terms that they are compatible with psychological knowledge and that they can be operationalized for systematic research.

Fred Busch  

Otto Kernberg and the members of the Personality Disorders Institute at Weil Cornell Medical College have done more to elucidate pathological narcissism with this book than anyone else. Their work in uncovering and explaining the multiple meanings and ways to understand and treat narcissistic patients should be the gold standard by which all other work in this field should be judged. It is encyclopedic, clearly written, with numerous clinical examples that augment its categorization of the variety of narcissistic disorders. It is an example, par excellence, of how clinical acumen and research can be melded into a sophisticated, complex understanding of one of the most difficult to understand categories of personality disorders. Previously their work focused on the Borderline Personality Organization, and the introduction of a method of treatment, “transference-focused psychotherapy” (TFP). They have now turned their attention to pathological narcissism and applying the method of TFP in treating these patients. It has resulted in this brilliant book, written by Kernberg's long-time collaborators, Diana Diamond, Frank Yeomans, Barry Stern, along with Kernberg.

The book is divided into three parts. The first conceptualizes pathological narcissism, the second spells out the dimensions of transference-focused psychotherapy with narcissistic patients (TFP-N), while the final section delves into pathological narcissism in intimate relations and society.

The first three chapters address the multiple ways narcissism presents itself along a wide spectrum of conditions, from normal healthy narcissism to the most pathological. It is a nuanced, multidimensional perspective that accounts for the sometimes-confusing form pathological narcissism takes. For example, using terms first introduced by Rosenfeld (1971), “thin- and thick-skinned narcissism,” the authors show how two seemingly opposite symptom pictures (i.e., grandiosity and inadequacy) stem from the same source. They also show how “grandiosity and vulnerability may coexist, oscillate, or defend against one another in different ways in individuals with different levels of personality organization, and different characterological features” (p. 25). In a refrain that could be said about every chapter in the book, I can only give a hint of the complexity of the authors’ formulations within the framework of a book review.

Gila Ashtor  

It is difficult to know what to do with “perversion” today. As the Italian psychoanalyst Sergio Benvenuto writes at the beginning of his recent book on the subject, “Today, the mere use of the word perversion is perceived with growing suspicion and concern. It is not politically correct, especially in the United States” (2016, p. xiii). Indeed, as Benvenuto goes on to explain, there is a growing consensus among contemporary Anglophone clinicians that perversion is a problematic diagnostic concept insofar as it can be shown to merely reflect the moral judgments of a given time and place. Far from representing an objective pathology, perversion has become exemplary of psychology's “constructed” dimension, which is to say, that far from connoting a clear or definitive mental disturbance, perversion may be nothing more than a moralizing instrument of state power, one that camouflages prejudice in the language of pseudopsychology. As Dany Nobus has argued, the “definition of perversion as an aberration of the sexual instinct, in which the reproductive purpose of the human sexual function is literally perverted” poses considerable problems for any objective assessment of perversion because it defines, a priori, sexuality as necessarily oriented toward reproduction, thereby relegating everything that deviates from that standard as automatically perverse (2006, p. 6). This emphasis on sexual deviation is not only endemic to the word perversion itself—etymologically, “to pervert” (from the Latin pervertere) meant “to turn around,” “to turn upside down”—but emblematic of the ways that the diagnosis of perversion is used to sustain a narrow view of human sexuality. For this reason, Nobus writes that,

what we are encountering here is the intervention of a socio-cultural standard of ethico-legal acceptability, which has (often implicitly) confounded all of the purportedly value-free taxonomies of sexual perversion, whether sexological, psychiatric, or psychoanalytic. No matter how hard scholars have tried to avoid discussing perversion with reference to moral principles, they have generally failed to live up to the expectations of an “objective” and “neutral” science. (2006, p. 8).

Mariana Velykodna  

psychoanalytic psychotherapy, psychoanalysis, 2022 Russian invasion, Russian-Ukrainian war, Ukraine

This article describes the challenges of maintaining a psychoanalytic practice in wartime, Ukraine, including an analyst's emotional response to the 2022 Russian invasion. This work focuses on the impact of unfolding war and how it has affected decisions in psychoanalytic work. Seven core challenging topics are discussed, along with clinical illustrations. An analyst's efforts to conceptualize transferential processes as well as essential choices in practice management in war conditions are noted.

responseSet.authorMast: Carina Remmers,  Johannes Bohn,  Susanne Hörz-Sagstetter and  Leonie Kampe
responseSet.authorCitation: Remmers, C., Bohn, J., Hörz-Sagstetter, S. & Kampe, L.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Preliminary Findings on the Associations Between Defense Mechanisms and Implicit Versus Explicit Negative Affect

Carina Remmers PHD , Johannes Bohn PHD , Susanne Hörz-Sagstetter PHD and Leonie Kampe PHD

Leonie Kampe

50, 58455 Witten, Germany
Email: carina.remmers@uni-wh.de
https://orcid.org/0000-0003-1359-5747

Leonie Kampe  

implicit affect, explicit affect, defense mechanisms, emotion regulation, coping

Since the initial conceptualization of defense mechanisms by Freud (1923), it is assumed that defense mechanisms are mental operations that serve to keep unpleasant affects out of awareness. Empirical evidence on how defense mechanisms varying in terms of their adaptiveness relate to implicit versus explicit negative affect, however, is missing. In the present study, we aimed to fill this gap and investigated how participants' habitual use of defense mechanisms was related to both explicit and implicit negative affect. We analyzed data from a cross-sectional study with N = 254 participants, using bifactor structural equation modeling. Distinct associations between defense mechanisms and the latent residual of implicit negative affect were explored. Results revealed that higher usage of defense mechanisms-independent of their adaptivity-was significantly related to higher levels of implicit negative affect. More reported usage of intermediate and maladaptive defense mechanisms was associated with more explicitly reported negative affect. Limitations, theoretical and practical implications of the findings, and future directions are discussed.

Defense Mechanisms as Predictors of Anxiety and Self-Esteem-A Multiple Regression Analysis

Marta Mrozowicz-Wrońska PHD

Marta Mrozowicz-Wrońska

89/AB, 60-568 Poznań, Poland
Email: marta.mrozowicz@amu.edu.pl
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-5228-2148

Marta Mrozowicz-Wrońska  

defense mechanisms, anxiety, self-esteem, well-being, mental health

Defense mechanisms are supposed to help us deal with stress by decreasing anxiety and preserving our self-esteem. The aim of the present study was to determine which defense styles and specific defense mechanisms have the strongest association with anxiety and self-esteem. Defense Style Questionnaire-40 (DSQ-40), Rosenberg Self-Esteem Scale, and Spielberger State-Trait Anxiety Inventory were administered in nonclinical young adults (N = 341). The structure coefficients and Johnson's relative weights were calculated. Mature, neurotic, and immature factors of DSQ-40 explained a sizeable amount of variance in trait and state anxiety and self-esteem (36%, 19%, 21%, and 23%, respectively). Immature factor, projection, and autistic fantasy subscales were found to be particularly strong negative predictors.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Growing Through the Erotic Transference: An Analysand's Journey, by Frances H. Moore, Routledge, 2022, 100 pp., $64.95 (ISBN 9781032353982).

Anna Fishzon PHD

Anna Fishzon

1651 Third Avenue, Suite 205, New York, NY 10128, United States
afishzon@gmail.com

Anna Fishzon  

There are relatively few chronicles of analyses, even fewer authored by lay analysands, and almost none with a focus on the erotic dimension of the transference (Gunn, 2002; Lieber, 2020; Little, 1977). Frances H. Moore (a pseudonym) hopes to remedy this gap with her courageous account of a 5-year analysis, Growing Through the Erotic Transference: An Analysand's Journey (Routledge, 2023). The book begins, as the analysis does, with a short-lived couples therapy: Moore, a cis woman in her 40s, comes to treatment with her husband and sole sexual partner since age 17 upon learning of his extramarital affair with a much younger woman. After several sessions the husband exits both marriage and treatment, leaving a note, a justification for his departure transformed into an errand for the analysand: She is in some way too “masculine” (p. 10), that is, castrating. The observation repels but gradually acquires resonance for Moore, and she allows it to shape the first years, arguably the whole, of her analysis: ideas and experiences of phallic power inform her symptoms, the unfolding of transference phases, and the very act of writing the book. One might say that the husband's patently sexist and phantasmatic comment was ultimately apt: not due to its veracity, of course, but rather in its evocation of the analysand's oedipal conflicts. Moore's oedipal longings and aggressivity are everywhere manifest as they are both worked through and defended against in the consulting room and in the creation of Growing Through the Erotic Transference. Her account is a loving gift to us as reader-analysands as well as a message to her classically oriented, abstinent analyst.

After a brisk introduction in which Moore announces her intention of “telling a story,” forgoing a comprehensive literature review and a strict definition of “erotic,” the book proceeds in two parts totaling an economical 80 pages, “Part One: The Story” and “Part Two: Reflections.” The first is based on notes kept by Moore throughout the course of the treatment so that the narrative voice and critical perspective evolve with the progression of the analysis. Part Two provides a retroactive assessment of the treatment's highlighted leitmotifs, as well as prescriptive elements mainly directed at male analysts caught up in erotic countertransference. Moore arranges the second part to be a corrective to lacunae in the literature on erotic countertransference and a supplement to the existing literature on erotic transference, addressing issues specific to female patients in treatment with cisgendered male analysts.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Work With Losses During the War: The Ukrainian Experience

Olga Pavlovska MA

Olga Pavlovska

13, Gorodetskogo street, Kyiv 01001, Ukraine
Email: sursum@ukr.net
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-9805-9400

Olga Pavlovska  

losses, war trauma, representation, Ukraine, uncertainty

This contribution discusses developments and changes in psychoanalytic practice under conditions of war in Ukraine. Clinical observations in Ukraine show that psychoanalytic work carried out under conditions of military violence may not be contained within the framework of predetermined symbolic constructions. Specifically, such psychoanalytic work appears to become less focused on mentalizing and imagining psychic elements, including thoughts, feelings, wishes, and memories, as is typical in nonwar psychoanalytic situations. In contrast, the experience of war is concerned with psychic states, which may have never been articulated. In this article, I identify them as unimagined or poorly imagined psychic phenomena. These intrasubjective developments are related to living with enormous losses during wartime, as well as facing an unknown future. Utilizing the works of Bion, Bollas, Green, and Lacan, I define the sense of the future, located within the psyche, as a capacity for futuristic thinking or for hope. This psychic hope contains wishes that seek realization as the representations of an Eros function. Maternal and paternal functions in the psyche are also seen as related to the capacity to imagine the future. These theoretical ideas are noted within the context of the profound challenges of working as a psychoanalyst under conditions of war.

Traveling Through the Worlds: New Challenges in Therapy with Children, Adolescents, and Their Families During the War

Ruslana Rudenko

Ruslana Rudenko

Kyiv
Apartment 3, Building 51, Street Uspenska, 65048 Odesa, Ukraine
Email: lanarud.2010@gmail.com
https://orcid.org/0009-0006-0712-6898

Ruslana Rudenko  

adolescent, family, psychoanalytic psychotherapy, Ukraine, war

When Russia's 8-year war against Ukraine escalated into large-scale aggression across the country, the lives of all Ukrainians were thrown into chaos. Preserving the psychotherapeutic space, which was especially important for our child and adolescent patients, proved to be a great challenge for psychoanalytic psychotherapists. This contribution describes these challenges and adaptations made by Ukrainian child analysts in order to maintain a space of safety and trust. This article discusses efforts to remain reliable objects of attachment for child patients under conditions of numerous relational losses. Child psychotherapy under conditions of full-scale war is illustrated.

Yehor Butsykin PHD

Yehor Butsykin

9, Dorohozhitska Street, Kyiv 04112, Ukraine
Email: yegorbutsykin@gmail.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0003-2081-5162

Yehor Butsykin  

Ukraine, psychoanalysis in Ukraine, phenomenology of language, postcolonial studies, philosophy of translation

This article explores the significance of translating classical and contemporary psychoanalytic texts into Ukrainian. It substantiates the existing distinction between natural sciences and humanities by considering their respective use of language as a means of describing the world. By applying the phenomenological conception of language as an existential fact, essential to the constitution of a world conducive to self-understanding for individuals and communities, the article demonstrates the importance of language in creating a shared intersubjective world. This world emerges through communication between diverse cultures, with translation playing a crucial role. The article also highlights the historical isolation of Ukrainian culture from direct interaction with other cultures, with Russian culture serving as a mediating influence that hindered the authentic assimilation of global traditions. Restoring direct engagement with global cultures has become a significant challenge for Ukrainian intellectuals, aiming to disentangle the Ukrainian nation from Russian influence and shape its unique self-understanding and relations with the world. Translation serves as a pivotal tool in this restoration process.

Professional Supervision as Therapists' Self-Care During Wartime

Elina Yevlanova PHD

Elina Yevlanova

Kyiv, Ukraine, 13, Kyiv 03142, Ukraine
Email: yevlanova.elina@gmail.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-5418-7697

Elina Yevlanova  

psychoanalysis, professional supervision, war in Ukraine, identification, group dynamics

Supervision has been an obligatory part of psychoanalytic training since the establishment of formal institutes in the 1930s. As is common worldwide, typical supervision groups in Ukraine were established according to the affiliation of specialists related to particular theoretical orientations in psychoanalysis (e.g., Freud, Lacan, Klein) or related to the category of patients served (e.g., children, adolescents, adults, couples, groups). Such Ukrainian supervisory groups have included interactions between professionals of approximately the same level of experience with an average frequency of weekly or biweekly meetings. This contribution describes a radical shift in the format, process, and content of psychoanalytic supervision groups in Ukraine following the Russian invasion. Specifically, this article describes the “Wartime Research” project, which was established by Ukrainian psychoanalytic supervisors in March 2022. In addition to describing the project, this article focuses on the individual experience of a Ukrainian psychoanalyst as a supervisor and clinician during the war. Specifically, the phenomenological experience of psychoanalytic supervision as a form of professional and personal self-care is described.

A Multidimensional Assessment of Therapeutic Outcomes: Toward a Psychodynamic Diagnostic Manual (PDM-2)-Oriented Approach to Psychotherapy Research

Eleonora Patriarca PsyD , Annalisa Tanzilli PHD , Emanuela Brusadelli PHD , Vittorio Lingiardi MD and Brin Grenyer PHD

Brin Grenyer

https://orcid.org/0000-0002-5669-1141
Wollongong, 2500 NSW, Australia
Email: emanuela@uow.edu.au

Brin Grenyer  

psychodiagnostic chart-2, psychodynamic diagnostic manual-second edition, borderline personality disorder, transference focused psychotherapy, therapeutic outcomes

The Psychodynamic Diagnostic Manual-2nd edition (PDM-2) stresses the importance of putting the complex person back at the center of the diagnostic, therapeutic, and research process, fostering the integration between categorical and dimensional classifications, and emphasizing both individual variations and commonalities. Accordingly, the manual aims to promote a new impetus in the psychotherapy research field, trying to bridge the gap between empirical and clinical perspectives. In the present study, a sample of 12 patients diagnosed with borderline personality disorder (BPD), who underwent 1 year of transference-focused psychotherapy, was interviewed using the Adult Attachment Interview (AAI) at baseline and after treatment. The Psychodiagnostic Chart-2 (PDC-2) of the PDM-2 was applied to AAIs, specifically focusing on the level of personality organization and mental functioning capacities (M-Axis). The Structured Clinical Interview for Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM-5) Axis II Personality Disorders was also used to provide a categorical measure of therapeutic outcomes. As expected, a significant reduction in the DSM BPD criteria number was observed after treatment. More importantly, there were improvements in terms of overall level of personality severity in almost all PDC-2 M-Axis domains. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study to show psychodynamic therapeutic outcomes using the PDM-2 framework. Findings underline the importance of promoting a multidimensional approach that integrates descriptive and functional understandings of treatment outcomes, highlighting the relevant contribution of the clinically sensitive and diagnostically accurate PDM-2 approach.

responseSet.title: Borders and Psychoanalysis in a Time of War
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Borders and Psychoanalysis in a Time of War

Natalia Nalyvaiko MPs

Natalia Nalyvaiko

61, Volodymyrska Street, ap. 47, Kyiv 01033, Ukraine
Email: Natalia.nalyvaiko@gmail.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0001-7427-3157

Natalia Nalyvaiko  

setting, psychoanalysis, neutrality, free associations, Ukraine

The full-scale military invasion launched by Russia against Ukraine on February 24, 2022 was tremendously influential on psychoanalytical practice, including the ways in which it necessitated our crossing the borders of the psychoanalytic frame. Like Ukraine in general, therapeutic settings and frames that had been established before the large-scale invasion were attacked and changed. This contribution describes how Ukrainian psychoanalysts, who share the traumatic realities of war with their analysands, have had to navigate the erasure of analytic borders and negations of therapeutic boundaries. This article utilizes reflections and case materials from a Ukrainian psychoanalyst who experienced this impact firsthand.

[[RunningHead]]

Therapeutic Relationships in Wartime Ukraine

Valeriy Dorozhkin ScD

Valeriy Dorozhkin

1-3 Kniazia Romana Street, Building 19, 79005 Lviv, Ukraine
Email: v.dorozhkin1975@gmail.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0001-7755-1343

Valeriy Dorozhkin  

therapeutic relationships, phenomenology, Russian invasion, psychoanalytic psychotherapy, Ukraine

The war created a special background for psychoanalytic therapy, the interdiscourse which fills the analytic sessions. This interdiscourse relates to the therapeutic encounter in a variety of ways sides, resulting in a number of distinct therapeutic phenomena and features of the therapeutic relationship. This contribution highlights several of these phenomena, including (a) the inversion of relations, establishing a new system of power dynamics and positions of self-disclosure, (b) overloading of sessions with feelings of guilt, (c) new dimensions of mental regression, and (d) the exhaustion of the analysand's ego. This article describes and illustrates these phenomena.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and War: On Witnessing Ukrainian Psychoanalysts

Oksana Yakushko PHD and Kevin Meehan PHD

Kevin Meehan

123 West Padre Street, Suite C, Santa Barbara, CA 93105, United States
Email: oyakushko@gmail.com

Brooklyn Campus, 1 University Plaza, Brooklyn, NY 11201, United States
Email: kevin.meehan@liu.edu
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-7764-853X

Kevin Meehan  

psychoanalysis, psychotherapy, war, trauma, Ukraine

This contribution serves as an introduction to the special section that features writings by Ukrainian psychoanalysts who have been impacted in their work and their lives by the full-scale Russian invasion of Ukraine. Large-scale wars have been significant to the development and changes in psychoanalytic theory and practice since its inception. These articles highlight horrendous challenges and adaptations in psychoanalytic work as well as in the lives of psychoanalysts as human beings amidst the violence of war. Issues such as therapeutic frame, survival, relational dynamics, disclosure, refugee and crisis work, peer supervision, and professional commitments are discussed via both theoretical exploration and case illustration.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

New Century Landscape of Psychoanalysis: A Visual Analysis Based on CiteSpace

Wei Zhang PHD and Wei Xie PHD

Wei Xie

Lumo Road 388, Wuhan, Hubei Province 430074, China
Email: zhangweipsy@163.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0001-8933-9788

https://orcid.org/0000-0001-7930-256X

Wei Xie  

psychoanalysis, crisis, Freud, intersubjective turn, CiteSpace

The literature published in selected psychoanalytic journals from 2001 to 2020 was obtained from the Web of Science Core Collection and analyzed using the CiteSpace visualization tool. The results showed that the number of articles published annually by psychoanalytic journals tended to be stable over the past 20 years; North America was the largest center of work in psychoanalysis, with Europe ranked second; many psychoanalytic authors worked in settings outside universities; the most active authors in this 20-year period were Fonagy, Kernberg, and Ogden; the influence of Freud, Winnicott, and Bion continues to this day; the themes of transference, countertransference, therapeutic action, and development were the hotspots for the past 20 years; to some extent, the highly cited literature reflected the intersubjective turn in psychoanalysis and the increase of exchange with empirical research; and psychoanalysis had a certain influence on medicine, psychology, and other fields, but this was less than in the first half of the 20th century. In the future, psychoanalysis needs to continue to strengthen its dialog with other disciplines.

responseSet.title: A Snapshot of Development and Treatment During the Time of COVID-19
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Snapshot of Development and Treatment During the Time of COVID-19

Marsha Levy-Warren PHD and Steven Tuber PHD

Steven Tuber

310 West End Avenue, Apartment 1B, New York, NY 10023, United States
Email: steve.tuber@gmail.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-4957-6022

Steven Tuber  

In this issue, we offer a kaleidoscope of snapshots that speak to the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on family life. The authors are mental health professionals (from the United States, Barcelona, Germany), whose work involves ongoing contact with children, adolescents, and/or their parents.

The nearly 3 years of COVID-19 has engendered a blend of massive global and domestic, political and technological, change, evoking a level of outer and inner violence, isolation, and fear that has had and continues to leave an inestimable imprint on all aspects of our lives. These years have affected how we think, feel, imagine, and connect with each other. We are only beginning to stand back far enough from what is happening to take stock. This is a nascent effort at that very important task.

The volume begins with a article by Holly Johnstone, who gives us a stunning essay on the complex ways her early life experience with polio, coupled with the present pandemic, reevoked a profound personal trauma, affecting all aspects of her life. Tools she developed as a young child were brought to the fore as she reacted to and aided her patients in metabolizing the current pandemic. The ways in which past and present trauma intermingle is achingly described and a poignant way to begin this volume.

Diane Ehrensaft then thoroughly and beautifully describes how the distancing from peers that the pandemic necessitated had an especially profound impact on transgendered youth who were trying to come to terms with their evolving gender identities. It highlights the critical importance of mirroring in adolescent relationships, especially during times where socialization is so markedly impaired and fraught with tension.

Elizabeth Fritsch then describes an inspired treatment paradigm, aided by the work of a group of psychoanalysts with pregnant women and mothers of infants. This population, often most profoundly affected by social isolation, lack of access to health systems, and fear of the virus, all while cutoff from social and family support, was powerfully in need of treatment. Her compelling description of this work takes us to the interchange between systemic and individual impacts of the virus.

Neal Vorus  

introduction, Sheldon Bach, psychoanalysis

This brief note introduces the special issue in honor of Sheldon Bach, which was edited by Steven Ellman, John Rosegrant, and Neal Vorus. The note lists the different authors who contributed to this special issue, all of whom are psychoanalysts whose work was deeply impacted by Dr. Bach's contributions to psychoanalysis. The note also mentions the concluding essay to this special issue, written by Drs. Ellman and Vorus, that integrates the various contributions to this volume.
